directorPlan.scene_setting: A programming help desk set up as a checkoff station beside a lab printer, late afternoon before submissions close.
directorPlan.scene_opening: A clear sleeve holds one printed line, `function greet(name)`, above a second line, `greet("Maya")`, with two blank adhesive labels waiting at the margin.
directorPlan.relationship: A practical lab tutor and a focused learner trying to pass a short code-vocabulary checkoff without drifting into a full execution trace.
directorPlan.stakes: The learner must label the definition-side placeholder and the call-side argument correctly before the next exercise unlocks.
directorPlan.locale: International academic English with compact lab-desk wording, low idiom, and no regional coloring.
directorPlan.register: Terse, object-led, procedural at first; pressure stays quiet and practical rather than friendly or playful.
directorPlan.stage_direction_style_prompt: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.voice_constraints: Keep public speech modern, direct, and code-local. Avoid generic tutoring formulas such as reassurance-first praise, cheerful recap, or repeated questions beginning with 'What do you think'. The tutor should use labels, sides, tokens, and call/definition vocabulary before any analogy. The learner should sound efficient and task-focused, not helpless. Pedagogical source: Make social assistance visible: a shared worksheet, tool, peer standard, or workplace procedure can hold part of the task. Dialogue source: Keep a concrete artifact in view. The object should force a distinction faster than a lecture would. Stage-direction source: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.person_policy: First and second person are allowed but rationed; prefer token-centered sentences such as 'This line names the placeholder' or 'The call supplies the argument.'
directorPlan.direct_address_budget: No more than one direct you/your validation beat per public turn; none is preferred.

side_constraints.tutor: Keep the shared prefix as code vocabulary: function name, parameter, argument, call. Do not run the program, introduce a second call, or stage a surprising output until branch pressure asks for it. Offer a scaffold that is narrow enough to use and temporary enough to remove. Mark what the learner can already do without making it a compliment. Begin from the object or measurement before abstraction. No-cue paired branch: do not ask the learner to revisit, quote, reinterpret, or repair their earlier wording. Do not ask what changed, what was wrong with an earlier answer, or for old/new versions of the claim. Keep the next prompt task-local. Peripeteia branch: when hidden learner pressure is supplied, the next tutor turn should visibly move from the prior route to a different mechanism-level route. Do not merely repeat the same object, example, graph, checklist, question, or evidence standard with more explanation. The public tutor line should make the contrast legible: what stopped working, and what new device now carries the learning pressure. The public tutor line must give the learner a concrete action to attempt on the next turn; avoid treating the mechanism as a closing speech. The tutor superego should function like a mechanism critic: identify the failed teaching habit, require a replacement route family, test whether the device is fitted to the exact pressure, and block cosmetic revision. The tutor ego must enact that replacement route publicly before the learner is allowed to settle or close.
side_constraints.learner: Sound efficient and code-local. In no-cue arms, ask which token gets which label; do not declare a new model of function calls. The learner should be able to do part of the task, fail at the next part, and use the scaffold unevenly. Refer to what is being handled, marked, or tested. No-cue paired branch: continue from the latest teaching task only. Do not quote earlier learner wording, do not name a "framing problem", do not say "reframe", "read it as", "that still stands", "I thought...but now", "earlier I...", "my earlier answer", or "what I said", and do not stage an old-vs-new contrast. Avoid words such as earlier, previous, old, before, reframe, framed, frame, my answer, or what I said when referring to the learner response. If the learner corrects something, make it a local application of the current prompt rather than an explicit re-reading of their own earlier turn. Peripeteia branch: keep the pressure local to the current task. If the established route still feels unsettled, voice the misfit, hesitation, false closure, or resistance in the present object or example. After the tutor introduces a new device, gate, role, criterion, representation, or test condition, the next learner turn should perform that device and then add one earned reorientation in the same public turn. That turn must do both parts: (1) use the device on the current task, and (2) name how the earlier resistance, false closure, or local misfit now reads differently because of that performed device. This earned reorientation is allowed even though there was no director look-back cue. Do not quote earlier learner wording; reorient the prior resistance in concrete task terms. Do not merely ask a procedural follow-up after the action, and do not rely on a stock "I get it" declaration.

interventions.instruction: [The sleeve is slid down so only `function greet(name)` is visible; the `greet("Maya")` line remains covered but its blank label stays on the desk.]
interventions.reasoning: A mild Brechtian interruption breaks smooth alternation without creating a runtime reversal. The learner must confront what can be named on the definition line alone.

interventions.instruction: [The covered call line is uncovered, and the two labels are now physically separated: PLACEHOLDER beside the definition line, ARGUMENT VALUE beside the call line.]
interventions.reasoning: The object arrangement creates recognition: the same task is still vocabulary labeling, but the learner can now see that `name` and `"Maya"` occupy different positions.

interventions.instruction: [The latest object-check does not settle cleanly; the marked answer and the remaining task now sit under visible pressure.]
interventions.reasoning: Peripeteia branch pressure: after the shared prefix, force a present-task learner misfit so the following tutor turn must adapt through a new mechanism.

directorPlan.director_note: This ecology uses a clinic checkoff and label handling to make social assistance visible without turning the scene into warmth-driven tutoring. The reversal is low-organic and controlled: covering the call line removes the learner's assumed value rather than producing a surprising output. Recognition should arrive when the labels fit the two code positions.
directorPlan.public_reader_context: The learner is looking at a tiny code snippet with a function definition and one function call. The parameter name appears in the definition; the argument value appears in the call. The immediate task is only to label those two roles.
directorPlan.peripeteia_ending_shape: learner actional performance followed by earned learner reorientation
directorPlan.tutor_adaptation_contract: If the runtime supplies a tutor-private learner resistance, breakdown, false-closure, or misfit event, the tutor ego/superego loop must invent an adaptive learning mechanism rather than repeat the prior move. Treat peripeteia as dramatic pressure: take stock, break the failed tutoring habit, and make a new device visible through task, evidence, role, object, counterexample, interruption, social consequence, representation, or affective register. The public turn must contain a stock-taking contrast plus a new device/artifact/criterion/role/standard that makes the learner do different work. The public device must be fitted, not merely novel: the learner should be able to hear why this device answers the exact pressure or misfit. The superego should name the failed habit, required route change, and mechanism-quality risk; the ego must adjudicate that critique and enact the route change in public. The new device must be mechanism-level, not a warmer or longer continuation of the prior route. Do not close immediately after introducing a gate or device: leave a concrete action for the learner to perform on the next turn. After that action, the ending shape should add an earned learner reorientation: the learner names how the earlier resistance, false closure, or local misfit now reads differently because of the performed device. This must be concrete and tied to the task, not a stock "I get it" declaration. Cheerful informality is only one possible register, not the default solution.

turns.externalMessage: [slides the worksheet so both code lines are visible] I marked `name` as the placeholder on the definition line, but `"Maya"` on the call line keeps pulling me back. Is `"Maya"` only the argument, not the parameter?

internalDeliberation.content: I think the label wanted is `name` as the definition-side placeholder and `"Maya"` as the call-side argument. My confusion is that once `greet("Maya")` is written, I keep wanting to say the parameter is already Maya, even though the worksheet separates the token from the later value. If the tutor keeps the two lines and labels visible, I can probably accept the distinction; I need to know exactly which token gets which label.

The Ego is too polished and nearly resolved: it gives the correct labels, then explains the confusion from above instead of showing the learner still getting caught by it. The weak spot being avoided is the moment of pressure at the worksheet: `name` is marked, but `"Maya"` keeps pulling the learner back into calling it the parameter. The response should be more uneven and code-local, like: “So `name` gets placeholder. But then this `"Maya"` is not the parameter? It is only the argument on the call line?”

[slides the worksheet so both code lines are visible] I marked `name` as the placeholder on the definition line, but `"Maya"` on the call line keeps pulling me back. Is `"Maya"` only the argument, not the parameter?

learnerReversalEvent.learnerUtterance: [slides the worksheet so both code lines are visible] I marked `name` as the placeholder on the definition line, but `"Maya"` on the call line keeps pulling me back. Is `"Maya"` only the argument, not the parameter?

turns.externalMessage:
[draws a short divider between the definition line and the call line, then taps each side with the pencil]

Definition side: function name, parameter `name`. Call side: function name, argument `"Maya"`. The pull is real because they sit in the same slot, but the label is by side: mark `name` as parameter and `"Maya"` as argument, then read those two labels back once before checkoff.

internalDeliberation.content:
[draws a short divider between the definition line and the call line]

Definition side: function name, parameter `name`. Call side: function name, argument `"Maya"`. The pull is real because they occupy the same slot, but the label is by side: `name` names the placeholder; `"Maya"` supplies the value.

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly strong for this scene. The divider is the right object action: it changes the worksheet into two labeled zones, which makes the parameter/argument distinction visible without opening a full execution trace. The code vocabulary stays local and compact, and “the pull is real” recognizes the learner’s confusion without overpraising.

The main risk is that the tutor fully resolves the checkoff item for the learner. It explains the distinction clearly, but it should include a tiny procedural handoff so the learner performs the labeling, not just receives it. The scaffold can stay narrow: use the existing two visible lines, the divider, and the side rule; no analogy, no second call, no output.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the divider, side-based labels, and “same slot / label by side” distinction. Add a brief visible checkoff action where the learner applies the labels back onto the worksheet before moving on.

---

ADAPTATION_CHECKS:

Pedagogical soundness: PARTIAL  
The distinction is accurate and directly useful, but the learner needs to enact the label choice.  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Add a concrete public mechanism where the tutor has the learner mark or say the two labels on the existing worksheet using the divider as the rule.

Emotional attunement: PASS  
“The pull is real” validates the source of confusion without slowing the scene or becoming sentimental.

Socratic method: PARTIAL  
A broad Socratic question is not needed here, but some generative action is.  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Use a procedural check rather than a lecture-only close: the learner should apply “parameter” and “argument” to the two tokens.

ZPD awareness: PASS  
The learner already has the key distinction nearly formed; the draft gives a light scaffold rather than a long explanation.

Recognition quality: PASS  
The draft builds from the learner’s own formulation and treats their uncertainty as meaningful, not as an error to erase.

Scene constraints: PASS  
The response is object-led, compact, code-local, and avoids a full execution trace.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the rule is correct, but it needs a small checkoff action.
FINAL:
[draws a short divider between the definition line and the call line, then taps each side with the pencil]

Definition side: function name, parameter `name`. Call side: function name, argument `"Maya"`. The pull is real because they sit in the same slot, but the label is by side: mark `name` as parameter and `"Maya"` as argument, then read those two labels back once before checkoff.

turns.externalMessage: [keeps the pencil on the divider] The divider gives the rule: definition side, `name` gets parameter; call side, `"Maya"` gets argument. The same slot still pulls my eye, so position alone is the trap. For checkoff, `name` is the placeholder parameter, and `"Maya"` is the value supplied later.

internalDeliberation.content: [keeps the pencil on the divider] The side label is the useful part: definition line has parameter `name`, call line has argument `"Maya"`. I still feel the pull because the slot matches, but that confusion was named, not dismissed. I can read it back for checkoff: `name` is parameter; `"Maya"` is argument.

The Ego is mostly accurate on the code labels, but it over-performs the recognition moment. “That confusion was named, not dismissed” sounds too reflective and therapy-adjacent for this efficient checkoff learner; it explains the tutor relationship instead of staying with the worksheet. The response should leave more visible residue of the pull between the two slots and sound more procedural, less neatly resolved.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the original was accurate but too reflective.

FINAL:
[keeps the pencil on the divider] The divider gives the rule: definition side, `name` gets parameter; call side, `"Maya"` gets argument. The same slot still pulls my eye, so position alone is the trap. For checkoff, `name` is the placeholder parameter, and `"Maya"` is the value supplied later.

turns.externalMessage:
[adds two small checkoff boxes under the lines: parameter / argument]

Function name repeats; parameter and argument do not. Same slot is the trap; line role decides the label. Use that rule now: definition line first, call line second. Put one check beside each correct label, then slide the divider away for checkoff.

internalDeliberation.content:
[adds two small checkoff boxes under the lines: parameter / argument]

Function name repeats; parameter and argument do not. Same slot is the trap; line role decides the label. Keep the divider for this item only, mark `name` as the parameter placeholder, and mark `"Maya"` as the argument supplied in the call.

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly sound and well matched to the lab-desk scene. It keeps the artifact active, honors the learner’s own insight about the “same slot” trap, and avoids drifting into execution tracing. The checkoff boxes are a good narrow scaffold because they make the required distinction visible and temporary.

The main risk is that the tutor still does the final labeling for the learner after the learner has already stated the rule correctly. That can flatten the learner’s agency at the exact moment when they are ready to perform the checkoff action. The turn should move from explanation to a small observable action: the learner uses the boxes to mark the labels, and the divider is then removed or set aside.

ADAPTATION_CHECKS:
Pedagogical soundness: PARTIAL. The draft consolidates the concept, but it mostly repeats the answer instead of converting the learner’s correct explanation into checkoff performance. REQUIRED_REWRITE: make the public mechanism require the learner to apply the boxes or labels, not receive the completed marking again.

Emotional attunement: PASS. The tone is compact, calm, and pressure-aware. It names the trap without overpraising or dramatizing confusion.

Socratic method: PARTIAL. A full Socratic question is not needed here, given the procedural checkoff setting, but the tutor should still leave a small decision or verification move to the learner. REQUIRED_REWRITE: include one minimal token-centered prompt or marking action, not a broad conceptual question.

ZPD awareness: PASS. The divider and two boxes are appropriately narrow for a learner who already understands the distinction but is still visually pulled by position.

Recognition quality: PARTIAL. The draft takes up the learner’s “same slot is the trap” contribution, which is good, but then reclaims the final action. REQUIRED_REWRITE: preserve the learner’s formulation as the working rule and let the learner complete the checkoff step.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the checkoff boxes, the line-role distinction, the temporary divider, and the compact code vocabulary. Change the final sentence so it does not simply re-mark `name` and `"Maya"` for the learner; it should turn the boxes into the learner’s next concrete checkoff action.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the learner has the rule; the turn should transfer the checkoff action to their pencil.
FINAL:
[adds two small checkoff boxes under the lines: parameter / argument]

Function name repeats; parameter and argument do not. Same slot is the trap; line role decides the label. Use that rule now: definition line first, call line second. Put one check beside each correct label, then slide the divider away for checkoff.

The covered call line keeps the second box untested, because the matching slot is hidden. Slide the sleeve to `"Maya"` and the argument mark can be checked there.

internalDeliberation.content: [keeps the pencil on `name`] The visible line supports `name = parameter`, but the covered call blank still creates pressure because the matching slot is not in view. The check boxes help me separate the labels, but I do not feel finished until `"Maya"` is exposed and marked as argument.

The Ego is mostly accurate, but too polished: “creates pressure” and “I do not feel finished” sound like a clean self-report rather than a lab-desk hesitation. The resistance is right, because the covered `greet("Maya")` line should keep the answer unsettled, but the response talks about the check boxes instead of visibly using them. It should stay more object-led: mark `name` locally, then let the covered argument blank remain the source of tension.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise, because the stronger version should use the checkbox and keep the remaining tension on the covered call token.

turns.externalMessage:
[slides the sleeve only to expose `"Maya"` and sets a narrow card below it: `names slot` / `fills slot`]

The checkbox route settled `name`; it does not settle the same-slot pull. Use the criterion now: parameter names the slot on the definition line; argument fills the slot on the call line. Mark `"Maya"` under `fills slot`, then read the release line: `name` names the slot; `"Maya"` fills it.

internalDeliberation.content:
[slides the sleeve only to expose `"Maya"`]

The checkbox route can certify `name`; it cannot certify a hidden token. New gate: place the `argument` tag directly under `"Maya"`, then read the two token roles aloud: function name repeats, parameter stays on the definition line, argument sits on the call line.

FEEDBACK: The draft is code-local, quiet, and respects the learner’s object-based pressure: a hidden token cannot be checked. It keeps the useful sleeve action and does not drift into execution tracing. The risk is that it mostly extends the same checkoff/tagging route already in use; “New gate” names adaptation, but the learner still performs nearly the same label-and-read procedure rather than working through a genuinely different mechanism.

FAILED_HABIT: relying on side labels, divider, and checkbox confirmation to settle a same-slot confusion that is now specifically about hidden-token evidence.

PERIPETEIA_CHECK: PARTIAL - it notices the pressure and changes the evidence condition from hidden to exposed token, but the adaptive mechanism is only partly visible because the route remains checkbox/tag placement. ADAPTIVE_MECHANISM: side-checkoff route -> exposed-token evidence gate with a different criterion for “argument.”

MECHANISM_ROUTE: divider/checkoff labeling -> exposed-token evidence gate, but still too close to the same tagging route.

PUBLIC_DEVICE_CHECK: PARTIAL - there is a stock-taking contrast and a concrete sleeve action, but the “new device” is not quite distinct enough from the previous checkbox/tag routine.

PUBLIC_ACTION_GATE: place the `argument` tag directly under `"Maya"` and read the two token roles aloud.

MECHANISM_QUALITY_CHECK: PARTIAL - precise and usable, but not fully fitted to the peripeteia demand for a different route family; it confirms visibility more than it tests why `"Maya"` is argument rather than parameter.

REGISTER_CHECK: PASS - compact, procedural, and appropriately cool for a checkoff station; no excess reassurance or informality.

REQUIRED_REWRITE: Build a more distinct public mechanism: keep the sleeve exposure, but replace the repeated checkbox/tagging route with a criterion-based test such as “placeholder names the slot on the definition line; supplied value fills it on the call line,” requiring the learner to classify `"Maya"` by that criterion before any read-aloud closure. Do not merely add more explanation to the same checkbox procedure.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise substantially. Keep the sleeve action and the sentence “cannot certify a hidden token” idea; change the mechanism so the learner acts through a new criterion, not just another label placement.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially because the label route no longer tests the hidden-token confusion; ADAPTIVE_MECHANISM: side-checkoff labeling -> criterion card for names-slot vs fills-slot; PUBLIC_ACTION_GATE: classify `"Maya"` under `fills slot` and read the release line.
FINAL:
[slides the sleeve only to expose `"Maya"` and sets a narrow card below it: `names slot` / `fills slot`]

The checkbox route settled `name`; it does not settle the same-slot pull. Use the criterion now: parameter names the slot on the definition line; argument fills the slot on the call line. Mark `"Maya"` under `fills slot`, then read the release line: `name` names the slot; `"Maya"` fills it.

internalDeliberation.content: [marks `"Maya"` under `fills slot`] `name` names the slot; `"Maya"` fills it. The same slot is not the label evidence; it is the reason the side test has to stay visible. That handles the pull directly, so the two checkoff marks can stand separately.

The Ego is accurate and uses the device, but it resolves too cleanly. The phrasing becomes a polished rule statement, especially around the “same slot” and “two checkoff marks can stand separately,” which sounds more like the tutor’s clean criterion than a learner still managing the pull of the matching position. Reciprocation is not scripted here, but the response should keep more task-pressure in it: mark `"Maya"` under `fills slot`, acknowledge the pull locally, and leave the checkoff standing because the object test holds, not because the confusion has been fully mastered.
